summary: DeVault challenges the free software community's biases by contrasting two tech speakers: Alyssa Rosenzweig, a trans woman who reverse-engineers GPU drivers for free software, and Scott Guthrie, a Microsoft VP. He argues that appearances and demographic assumptions lead the community to unfairly scrutinize leaders who don't fit the white male norm, while people like Rosenzweig contribute enormously to the movement. The post rejects the notion that diversity advocacy is a 'woke agenda' and argues that free software is inherently political, making respect and inclusion inseparable from the mission.
key_points: Demographic assumptions cause the community to unfairly judge leaders who don't match the white male default, regardless of their technical merit
Alyssa Rosenzweig's contributions to free GPU drivers far more closely align with FOSS values than a Microsoft VP, yet she faces identity-based harassment
The homogeneity of the free software community is a result of ostracism, not predisposition for the work
Free software is inherently political, making 'forget politics, focus on software' an impossible and dishonest demand
Diverse leadership brings new perspectives that strengthen both the software and the movement
money_quotes: You and I have a lot more in common with Alyssa than with Scott.
Is it any wonder that our community is predominantly made up of white cisgendered men when anyone else is ostracized?
It's patently absurd to suppose that hackers whose identities and life experience differ from yours or mine cannot be good participants in and leaders of our movement.
The free software ecosystem (and the world at large) is not under threat from some woke agenda – a conspiracy theory which has been fabricated out of whole cloth.
Forget the politics and focus on the software? It's simply not possible. Free software *is* politics.

Treating other people with respect, maturity, and professionalism, and valuing their contributions at any level, including leadership, regardless of their appearance or identity – that's just part of being a good person. *That* is apolitical.

Asking them to shut up and get in line, to suppress their identity, experiences, and politics, to avoid confronting you with uncomfortable questions about your biases and privileges by way of their existence alone – it's not right.
key_insight: Free software is inherently political, and welcoming diverse leaders strengthens both the community and its mission far more than enforcing a false apolitical conformity.
